summary: An auction listing of historical letters between Walt Disney, his staff, and music critic Deems Taylor. The correspondence reveals Disney's creative philosophy during the development of Fantasia and Alice in Wonderland, specifically his belief in reinterpreting classic works and making them accessible to the general public.

principles.statement: It is our function to put new wine in old bottles.
principles.rationale: Creators have the right to reinterpret classic works in their own way. Even if there is initial resistance, the public will accept the new interpretation once they are presented with the finished product.
principles.source_quote: Walt maintains that it is our function to put new wine in old bottles and that we are perfectly within our rights in interpreting this symphony in our own way - that once the public is confronted with a "fait accompli," they will accept it.

principles.statement: Classic stories should be adapted for the masses, not put on a pedestal.
principles.rationale: Treating a classic work with too much reverence alienates ordinary people and prevents them from getting something meaningful out of it.
principles.source_quote: I believe the thing that has kept the people from going for ALICE IN WONDERLAND has been the fact that it has always been put on a pedestal. It hasn't yet been treated in a way that lets the ordinary people get something out of it. Its potentialities are great but I definitely feel it should be made for the masses.

mental_models.name: New Wine in Old Bottles
mental_models.description: A lens for creative adaptation: taking established, classic art (the "old bottle") and reinterpreting it through a modern, innovative medium or perspective (the "new wine") to create something fresh.
mental_models.example: Interpreting Beethoven's "Pastorale" symphony through groundbreaking animation in Fantasia, despite the misgivings of traditionalists.

signature_quotes.text: Walt maintains that it is our function to put new wine in old bottles and that we are perfectly within our rights in interpreting this symphony in our own way - that once the public is confronted with a "fait accompli," they will accept it.
signature_quotes.context: Reported by Disney casting director Stuart Buchanan, describing Walt's defense of their unconventional treatment of Beethoven's Pastorale symphony in Fantasia.

signature_quotes.text: I believe the thing that has kept the people from going for ALICE IN WONDERLAND has been the fact that it has always been put on a pedestal. It hasn't yet been treated in a way that lets the ordinary people get something out of it.
signature_quotes.context: From a 1942 letter to Deems Taylor discussing the creative direction for an Alice in Wonderland feature.

signature_quotes.text: Its potentialities are great but I definitely feel it should be made for the masses.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the adaptation of Alice in Wonderland.

signature_quotes.text: Perhaps there is a place in the movies for the classics after all.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the slow but steady repeat box office returns for Fantasia in 1943.

anti_patterns.description: Putting classic literature or art "on a pedestal."
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents ordinary people from connecting with the material and limits the property's popular appeal.
